input.background: In Exercise 9.3, you tested $H_0: \mu=50$ based on a sample of $n=36$ showing $\bar{x}=48.8$. The population had standard deviation $\sigma=7.5$.
input.data:
Patient satisfaction. Scores derived from a patient satisfaction survey are Normally distributed with $\mu=50$ and $\sigma=7.5$, with high scores indicating high satisfaction. An SRS of $n=36$ is taken from this population.
(a) What is the $S E$ of $\bar{x}$ for these data?
(b) We seek evidence against the hypothesis that a particular group of patients come from a population in which $\mu=50$. Sketch the curve that describes the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}$ under the null hypothesis. Mark the horizontal axis with values that are $\pm 1, \pm 2$, and $\pm 3$ standard errors above and below the mean.
(c) Suppose a sample of $n=36$ finds an $\bar{x}$ of 48.8 . Mark this finding on the horizontal axis of your sketch. Then calculate the $z_{	ext {stat }}$ for the result. Does this observation provide strong evidence against $H_0$ ?
input.question: Convert the $z_{\text {stat }}$ to a $P$-value, and interpret the results.

output.answer: $P=\operatorname{Pr}(Z<-0.96)=0.1685$. Interpretation: If $H_0$ were correct, results this extreme or more extreme would occur about $17 \%$ of the time (that is, would not be that unusual).

mu.description: The population mean satisfaction score under the null hypothesis.

sigma.description: The population standard deviation of satisfaction scores.

n.description: The sample size of the simple random sample (SRS).

SE.description: The standard error of the sample mean, calculated as sigma divided by the square root of n.

z_stat.description: The z-statistic, calculated to test the null hypothesis.

P_value.description: The P-value corresponding to the z-statistic, used to interpret the strength of evidence against the null hypothesis.
